title: Legalism and Daoism
description: Let's revisit that article from earlier and dive into Legalism and Daoism.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the following passage and answer the question. 

> "A fanatical focus on conscripting troops and increasing agricultural production turned the state of Qin into a military powerhouse by the 3rd century BCE; the young King Zheng began a nine-year campaign to conquer his neighbors, and in 221, when his opponents lay in ruins, Zheng declared himself Qin Shi Huang, first Emperor of Qin. The emperor set about creating an administrative bureaucracy fashioned after his home state: China was divided up into regional administrative zones, all under the watchful eyes of Qin Empire officials. Under Qin Shi Huang, common people were conscripted into forced labor and punished or disfigured for petty infractions."

Khan Academy, "The Philosophers of the Warring States"

**How did Legalism enable Qin Shi Huang to enact his imperial policies?** 


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: While Qin Shi Huang's government forced peasants into agricultural and military servitude, it would not be accurate to say that Legalism encourages "quiet lives of agrarianism".
Qin Shi Huang's administrators and bureaucrats enforced his will; they did not serve to keep the emperor in check.

choices.content: Legalist doctrine allowed the emperor to centralize command over state resources
Under Legalism, the emperor was held in check by his many ministers
Legalism's focus on justice made Qin Shi Huang popular among the common folk
Legalist doctrine encourages quiet lives of agrarianism; this increased the productivity of farming households

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the following passage and answer the question. 

> "A fanatical focus on conscripting troops and increasing agricultural production turned the state of Qin into a military powerhouse by the 3rd century BCE; the young King Zheng began a nine-year campaign to conquer his neighbors, and in 221, when his opponents lay in ruins, Zheng declared himself Qin Shi Huang, first Emperor of Qin. The emperor set about creating an administrative bureaucracy fashioned after his home state: China was divided up into regional administrative zones, all under the watchful eyes of Qin Empire officials. Under Qin Shi Huang, common people were conscripted into forced labor and punished or disfigured for petty infractions."

Khan Academy, "The Philosophers of the Warring States"


**How might the chaos of the Warring States Period explain the emergence of Daoism?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Respecting your elders is a principle of Confucianism.
Qin Shi Huang never renounced violence; you might be thinking of the Mauryan emperor Ashoka.

choices.content: Qin leaders wanted a religious and philosophical system that would complement their extensive bureaucracy
Daoism was the religion adopted by Qin Shi Huang; after his bloody nine-year campaign, he renounced violence and sought a more peaceful philosophy
Daoism’s principles of inaction and living simply may have been a reaction to the fighting and strict punishments of the Warring States Period
Daoism’s principles of respecting one’s elders may have been a reaction to the bloodshed and strict laws of the Warring States Period
